title: A menu that is not a menu
testCase: Did you turn the menu into a <nav> with a list inside?
blocks.0.text: You made a menu. Three links, separated by bars. Run it — it works, it looks like a menu. All good.
blocks.1.text: Except now look at the code and ask yourself: what here says that this is a menu?
blocks.2.text: Nothing. There is a paragraph with three links and two bars. You put the bars there so it would look like a menu — but they are a drawing, not meaning. To a machine this is a paragraph that happens to contain links. The same as a sentence with a link in the middle.
blocks.3.text: The same thing again: you put punctuation where a tag belongs.
blocks.5.text: Stop and think about what a menu IS. Several equal things, each leading somewhere. Their order does not matter. You already know this tag — it is <ul>.
blocks.6.text: And around it: <nav>. It does nothing visible. It says: here is the site navigation.
blocks.10.text: Exactly. It is stacked, bulleted, and takes half the screen. The first version looked better. And still the second one is right.
blocks.11.text: Because HTML is not about how things look. It says WHAT they are. Making the menu lie flat, without bullets, with spacing — that is CSS, and it is five minutes of work. But if there is no list inside, CSS has nothing to arrange.
blocks.13.items.0: A blind visitor — their screen reader announces "navigation, 3 links" and they skip it. With a paragraph they would sit through all of them, on every page.
blocks.13.items.1: Google — sees where the menu is and understands the structure of your site.
blocks.13.items.2: You, six months from now — you open the code and immediately know what is what.
blocks.14.text: <nav> is for the MAIN navigation. Do not put it around every link. Once or twice per page — the menu at the top, maybe some links at the bottom.
blocks.15.text: Your turn. Make the menu what it actually is: navigation with a list of links. Drop the bars.
